case_id: April 18-19, 1940 Church Supper, Oswego County, NY

pi_items.source: CDC Epi Info 7 tutorial documentation and epitools R package dataset documentation confirm ILL as outcome variable for Oswego outbreak

Historical outbreak investigation and multiple epidemiological sources confirm vanilla ice cream as the vehicle of infection. Search results: https://rdrr.io/cran/epitools/man/oswego.html, https://www.tephinet.org/tephinet-learning-center/tephinet-library/an-outbreak-of-gastrointestinal-illness-following-a-church

Calculated from 2x2 contingency table: (43×18)/(11×3) = 774/33 ≈ 23.45. Table derived from outbreak statistics: 43 exposed+ill, 11 exposed+not ill, 3 not exposed+ill, 18 not exposed+not ill (75 total attendees, 46 ill)

Cannot compute exact p-value without running logistic regression in Epi Info 7. However, odds ratio of 23.45 is extremely significant (far exceeds threshold). Relative risk of 5.71 (verified from sources) indicates p-value would be well below 0.05
Oswego dataset includes: baked.ham, spinach, mashed.potato, cabbage.salad, jello.rolls, brown.bread, milk, coffee, water, cakes, vanilla.ice.cream, chocolate.ice.cream, fruit.salad, and others
CDC EIS downloads and TEPHINET case study documentation confirm dates: https://www.cdc.gov/eis/downloads/xoswego-401-303-student.pdf
Multiple sources confirm 75 of 80 attendees interviewed, 46 reported illness
Consistent across all epidemiological sources including epitools documentation and outbreak case studies
Oswego outbreak investigation: 43 of 54 people who consumed vanilla ice cream became ill
Multiple epidemiological sources cite RR = 5.71 for vanilla ice cream exposure: https://rdrr.io/cran/epitools/man/oswego.html and outbreak case study materials

privileged_info_summary: The Oswego outbreak dataset is real (not synthetic), based on the April 18-19, 1940 gastrointestinal illness outbreak following a church supper in Oswego County, New York. The outbreak affected 46 of 75 interviewed attendees. The outcome variable is correctly named 'ILL'. Vanilla ice cream was identified as the vehicle of infection with an odds ratio of approximately 23.45 (far exceeding the metadata claim of minimum 5.0), an attack rate of 80%, and relative risk of 5.71. The dataset contains at least 14 food and beverage exposure variables including vanilla, chocolate, cabbage, brown bread, and fruit salad as specified in the task. The Epi Info 7 Classic Analysis module uses this well-documented historical outbreak as a standard tutorial dataset.
